author: y4rr
content:
Landed.

## What landed

### 8 cubecl kernels in \`ferrotorch-cubecl/src/kernels.rs\`
\`#[cube(launch_unchecked)]\` kernels â€” runtime-generic so they compile to PTX (cuda), HIP (rocm), and WGSL (wgpu):
- \`kernel_chebyshev_t\`, \`kernel_chebyshev_u\`, \`kernel_chebyshev_v\`, \`kernel_chebyshev_w\`
- \`kernel_hermite_h\`, \`kernel_hermite_he\`
- \`kernel_laguerre_l\`, \`kernel_legendre_p\`

Each thread runs the same three-term recurrence as the existing CPU evaluators in \`ferrotorch-core/src/special.rs\` â€” one input element per thread, recurrence iterated up to degree \`n\`, single write at the end. No host bounce inside the kernel.

### Runner / dispatch layer
- New \`run_unary_with_n\` helper for kernels taking a single \`u32\` scalar argument
- 8 \`run_<family>\` runners stamped via macro (\`define_unary_with_n_runner!\`)
- 8 \`portable_<family>(x, n, rt)\` public APIs in \`ops.rs\` via macro (\`define_portable_polynomial!\`)
- New \`dispatch_unary_with_n!\` macro for the wgpu/cuda/rocm runtime selection

### 11 integration tests (\`ferrotorch-cubecl::ops::tests\`)
Verify against closed-form polynomial identities:
- T_3(x) = 4xÂ³ - 3x ; U_2(x) = 4xÂ² - 1 ; V_1(x) = 2x-1 ; W_1(x) = 2x+1
- H_3(x) = 8xÂ³ - 12x ; He_3(x) = xÂ³ - 3x
- L_2(x) = Â½(xÂ² - 4x + 2) ; P_2(x) = Â½(3xÂ² - 1)
- Edge cases for n=0 (returns 1) and n=1 (returns base case)
- Multi-cube launch test (1024 elems > 256 cube dim)

All 11 tests pass on wgpu backend in WSL2 (cargo test -p ferrotorch-cubecl --features wgpu). Cuda backend builds clean.

## Out of scope (intentionally)
- Wiring \`ferrotorch-core::special::*\` to dispatch through cubecl when input is on GPU. That would require a circular dep (\`special\` would need to know about \`CubeRuntime\`). Current pattern matches existing portable_* ops: users pick the cubecl path explicitly.
- Shifted variants (T*, U*, V*, W* on [0,1]) â€” trivial wrappers over the base kernels, can land later if there's demand.
- f64 polynomial kernels â€” cubecl's f64 support is uneven across backends; f32 is the cross-backend baseline.
